coredata.dc:title: Chapter 5 Chemical Nanosensors in Pharmaceutical Analysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: New Developments in Nanosensors for Pharmaceutical Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2019 Sibel A. Ozkan and Afzal Shah. Published by Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Kurbanoglu, Sevinc
Bakirhan, Nurgul K.
Shah, Afzal
Ozkan, Sibel A.
coredata.dc:description: Nanosensors have improved very quickly in the last decades and their roles have greatly expanded within the pharmaceutical and biomedical fields, in which measurement science and technology are of key importance. The enhanced chemical and physical properties arising from discrete nanoelectrode devices or the modification of the electrode surface with nanomaterials make these devices an attractive option for the improvement of current analytical applications in the pharmaceutical area because of their excellent sensitivity. Chemical nanosensors can be designed using carbon-based (fullerene carbon nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamond, etc.), polymer-based, or metal-based (gold, silver, platinum nanoparticles, etc.) nanomaterials. Chemical nanosensors are generally used to detect various chemicals in monitoring pollution, in drugs, in residue from environmental samples, and for the assay of organophosphorus compounds. Moreover, chemical nanosensors are also used for diagnostic purposes. A transducer, such as an electrochemical, optical, thermal, or piezoelectric transducer, combined with the sensor is used in chemical nanosensors for pharmaceutical analysis. This chapter is focused on chemical nanosensors and their applications in pharmaceutical analysis with different types of samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanosensors
Carbon based nanomaterials
Metal based nanomaterials
Pharmaceutical analysis

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) DPVs of bare and modified GCE in the absence (dotted lines) and presence (full lines) of a mixture of AA, UA, and Ser; 5μM each and Epn 10μM in PBS (pH 7.4). (B) DPVs of bare (a) and modified GCE (b–e) in PBS (pH 7.4) containing a mixture of AA, UA, and Ser; 5μM each along with epinephrine at different concentrations of 4 (b), 6 (c), 8 (d), 10 (a, e) μM.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission of the publisher Elsevier (Koteshwara Reddy, K., Satyanarayana, M., Yugender Goud, K., Vengatajalabathy Gobi, K., & Kim, H. (2017). Carbon nanotube ensembled hybrid nanocomposite electrode for direct electrochemical detection of epinephrine in pharmaceutical tablets and urine. Materials Science and Engineering C, 79, 93–99. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.05.012).

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of deposited Pt:Co NPs. (B) Histogram of the size distribution of Pt:Co NPs.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission of the publisher Elsevier (Jamali, T., Karimi-Maleh, H., & Khalilzadeh, M. A. (2014). A novel nanosensor based on Pt: Co nanoalloy ionic liquid carbon paste electrode for voltammetric determination of vitamin B9in food samples. LWT—Food Science and Technology, 57(2), 679–685. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.lwt.2014.01.023).

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic principle of GSH detection using a dual-mode nanosensor with both colorimetric and fluorometric readout. TEM (A) and HRTEM (B) images of the as-prepared CQDs. The inset of (B) is the SAED pattern. (C) The UV-vis absorption spectrum of the CQDs and of the AuNPs and the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of the CQDs. The inset is a photograph of the CQDs under visible light and 365 nm UV light, from left to right, respectively. (D, E) XPS spectra of the CQDs: C 1s spectrum (D) and N 1s spectrum (E) of the CQDs. (F) FTIR spectra of AuNPs and CQDs.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission of the publisher Elsevier (Shi, Y., Pan, Y., Zhang, H., Zhang, Z., Li, M. J., Yi, C., & Yang, M. (2014). A dual-mode nanosensor based on carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles for discriminative detection of glutathione in human plasma. Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 56, 39–45. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2013.12.038).

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics of the detection principle using aptamer-associated gold nanosensors with SYBR Green-I (SG-I) for the sensitive detection of bisphenol A (BPA).

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission of the publisher Elsevier (Lee, E. S., Kim, G. B., Ryu, S. H., Kim, H., Yoo, H. H., Yoon, M. Y., … Kim, Y. P. (2018). Fluorescing aptamer-gold nanosensors for enhanced sensitivity to bisphenol A. Sensors and Actuators, B: Chemical, 260, 371–379. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.01.018).

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 5

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) (a) SEM image of MgO/NPs. (b) TEM image of MgO/NPs. (c) EDX pattern of as-synthesized MgO nanoparticles, indicating the atomic percentage of Mg and O. (B) Cyclic voltammograms of (a) MgO/NPs/IL/CPE, (b) MgO/NPs/CPE, (c) IL/CPE, and (d) CPE in the presence of 200μmolL−1 methyldopa at pH 7.0. Inset: the current density derived from cyclic voltammogram responses of 200μmolL−1 methyldopa at pH 7.0 at the surface of different electrodes with a scan rate of 50mVs−1.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission of the publisher Elsevier (Vahedi, J., Karimi-Maleh, H., Baghayeri, M., Sanati, A. L., Khalilzadeh, M. A., & Bahrami, M. (2013). A fast and sensitive nanosensor based on MgO nanoparticle room-temperature ionic liquid carbon paste electrode for determination of methyldopa in pharmaceutical and patient human urine samples. Ionics, 19(12), 1907–1914. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11581-013-0940-z).

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of theophylline-templated AgNP@MIP nanocomposites and subsequent SPE of caffeine prior to SERS detection.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission of the publisher Elsevier (Hu, R., Tang, R., Xu, J., & Lu, F. (2018). Chemical nanosensors based on molecularly-imprinted polymers doped with silver nanoparticles for the rapid detection of caffeine in wastewater. Analytica Chimica Acta, 6–13. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.06.012).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanosensors have improved very quickly in the last decades and their roles have greatly expanded within the pharmaceutical and biomedical fields, in which measurement science and technology are of key importance. The enhanced chemical and physical properties arising from discrete nanoelectrode devices or the modification of the electrode surface with nanomaterials make these devices an attractive option for the improvement of current analytical applications in the pharmaceutical area because of their excellent sensitivity. Chemical nanosensors can be designed using carbon-based (fullerene carbon nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamond, etc.), polymer-based, or metal-based (gold, silver, platinum nanoparticles, etc.) nanomaterials. Chemical nanosensors are generally used to detect various chemicals in monitoring pollution, in drugs, in residue from environmental samples, and for the assay of organophosphorus compounds. Moreover, chemical nanosensors are also used for diagnostic purposes. A transducer, such as an electrochemical, optical, thermal, or piezoelectric transducer, combined with the sensor is used in chemical nanosensors for pharmaceutical analysis. This chapter is focused on chemical nanosensors and their applications in pharmaceutical analysis with different types of samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Sensors are analytical devices that selectively and reversibly respond to the amount or activity of a chemical agent in a sample. Chemical sensors are generally used in qualitative or quantitative determination of the analytes of concern. In principle, the analyte is sensed by the surface of the chemical sensor, which is the result of a simple physical or chemical interaction. This interaction is converted into a steady electrical signal that can be measured by a device called a transducer. The output signal mirrors the amount of analyte in the sample. Such signal transformation methods are generally based on acoustic, optical, thermal, and electrochemical considerations. The sensor converts chemical or physical properties into useful information. According to this definition, a chemical sensor is a device that generates an electrical signal as a function of the chemical nature or concentration of an analyte molecule. An ideal chemical sensor should be small in size, simple, cheap, and reliable (Chen & Chatterjee, 2013; Fojta, 2002; Hu et al., 2010; Janata, 2009; Bonizzoni & Anslyn, 2009; Xueji, Huangxian, & Joseph, 2007).

The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry defines a chemical sensor as, “A device that transforms chemical information, ranging from the concentration of a specific sample component to total composition analysis, into an analytically useful signal. The chemical information, mentioned above, may originate from a chemical reaction of the analyte or from a physical property of the system investigated” (Janata, 2009).
Some parameters are required to exhibit the best measurement in chemical sensors.

The characteristic behaviors of an ideal chemical sensor are given below.
•
Sensitivity: The lowest change in the analytical device (in terms of concentration) to which the sensor can respond.
•
Selectivity: The sensor refers only to the validity of the material to be measured and the sensor does not show any interest for other materials in the environment. Then, one can say that the sensor is selective.
•
Measurement range: The analyte concentration that the sensor can convert to electronic data. If the analyte is less than or more than a certain concentration value, it may not be able to be measured with good sensitivity.
•
Measurement time: It is the time of the sensor response to changes in the concentration of the analyte.
•
Repeatability: The same analytical measurement results of the device are comparable with each other.
•
Detection limit: The lowest analyte concentration the sensor can measure.
•
Shelf-Life Stability: Without increasing the error values in the measurements of the sensor, repeatable measurements within a certain period of time (in weeks or months) are measured.

Chemical sensors have two parts: a receptor and a transducer. The receptor part obtains the chemical information by the related chemical reaction and transfers it to the transducer part to convert it into a measurable signal. Problems with the detection of pharmaceuticals with a normal electrode can be resolved with a chemically modified electrode system. In many analytical processes, chemical sensors are used to monitor the activity or concentration of the respective chemical species in the gas or liquid phase. Therefore, chemical sensors find use in clinical diagnostics, environmental pollution monitoring, and food and drug analysis, which require continuous chemical analysis (Chen & Chatterjee, 2013; Fojta, 2002; Hu et al., 2010; Janata, 2009; Bonizzoni & Anslyn, 2009; Xueji et al., 2007).

The most important part of the chemical sensor structure is its sensor, which is also called the recognition layer. The recognizing layer generally identifies with the specific chemical energy pathways that are present in the structure of the material that it wishes to recognize. The task of the translation is to turn the signal from the spot in the recognizing layer into a measurable energy. Finally, the analytical system is supported by electronic units, such as preamplifiers, impedance transducers, multiplexers, and analog-to-digital converters, placed directly behind the transducer. What should be in this electronic unit is to avoid magnetic fields and electric fields that can be dealt with outside the system. Depending on the transducer part, chemical nanosensors can be classified as electrochemical nanosensors, optical nanosensors, thermal nanosensors, or piezoelectric nanosensors.

In piezoelectric nanosensors, piezoelectric materials and surface acoustic wave devices provide a sensitive environment for mass change. These types of transducers are ideal for use when mass exchange occurs after a recognition reaction. For example, quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) piezoelectric silicon crystals are able to perceive mass changes even at the picogram level on the surface of the sensor. Piezoelectric sensors are gaining more attention as they were very unusual materials with very particular and interesting properties. Actually, they have the potential to produce electrical energy from mechanical energy or, to be clearer, they can convert vibrations into electricity. Such devices are generally referred to as energy harvesters and can be utilized in applications for which outside power is unavailable and batteries are not a feasible option. Simultaneously, as recent experiments have proven, these materials could be used as power generators; however, they produce a small amount of energy, which places an emphasis on improving the piezoelectric material (Lao, Kuang, Wang, Park, & Deng, 2007; Saini, Bagri, & Bajpai, 2017; Xu, Poirier, & Yao, 2012; Zhao, Wang, & Mao, 2004). In thermal nanosensors, the assay is performed by measuring the change in ambient temperature during the chemical reactions (Eaton & Smith, 1997; Lee et al., 2018; Mandal & Song, 2015; Van Herwaarden & Sarro, 1986). In optical sensors, the signal occurs as a result of light reflection or scattering. Optical-based sensors basically operate within the framework of such basic principles as absorption, fluorescence, or bioluminescence (Bhattacharya, Singha, & Basu, 2017). Optical sensor devices convert the receiving part and the resulting optical events into analyte interactions. These chemical sensors can also be classified according to the type of optical properties applied (Bhattacharya et al., 2017; Bühlmann, Ernö, & Bakker, 1998; Homola, Yee, & Myszka, 2008; Krioukov, Klunder, Driessen, Greve, & Otto, 2002; Lee, 2003; Li, Cushing, & Wu, 2015; McFarland & Van Duyne, 2003; Rajendran & Ellington, 2002; Sun, Bebis, & Miller, 2004);
•
Absorbance due to reaction of the analyte with the appropriate indicator or absorbance of the analyte itself in a transparent environment.
•
Reflectance measured in a nontransparent environment, usually using an immobilized indicator.
•
Luminescence based on measuring the intensity of the emitted light after the chemical reaction occurs in the receiving system.
•
Fluorescence, measured by the emission of radiation. Also, selective fluorescence quenching can be based on such devices.
•
Refractive index measured after a change in solution composition.
•
Optothermal effect based on the measurement of the thermal effect caused by light absorption. Light scattering based on the effects caused by particles of a certain magnitude.

In the literature, there exist mainly electrochemical sensors, which determine the amount of analyte by means of a change in the potential, current, capacitance, conductivity, and resistance, formed by a specific chemical reaction. Therefore, in this chapter, electrochemical nanosensors that employ the most widely used methods and electrodes are discussed.

Electrochemical reactions are redox-type reactions that take place in a cell called the “electrochemical cell.” The electrochemical reactions consist of a solution or molten salt, called the supporting electrolyte, which contains the substance for investigation. Electrochemical methods are divided into two classes called bulk and interfacial. In the bulk method, all properties of the solution and the analyte are examined. In the interfacial method, the interactions at the electrode and solution interface are evaluated. The electrochemical methods that occur at the interface can also be divided into two methods, the dynamic and statistic methods (Brett, 1993; Brown, Large, Weissberg, & Rossiter, 1964; Gosser, 1994; Greef, Peat, Peter, Pletcher, & Robinson, 1990; Hart, 1990; Ozkan, 2012; Ozkan, Kauffmann, & Zuman, 2015; Palacek, Scheller, & Wang, 2005; Scholz et al., 2010; Vire & Kauffmann, 1994; Wang, 2006; Zhang, Ju, & Wang, 2008).

In voltammetry, the current between the working electrode and the opposing electrode is measured against the tensile strain, which is applied between a microworking electrode and a comparative electrode, whose current value is very small in the cell. It is based on the current-potential relationship. The graph drawn against the measured current values of the applied voltage is called the voltammogram. In voltammetry, solid electrodes are used as working electrodes. If a mercury electrode is used in experiments, the method is called polarography. The measurements can be operated between +0.5V and −3.0V with a mercury electrode. If one works at a higher value of potential than these values, the mercury is oxidized. If one works at lower values, H+ begins to be reduced and H2 gas exits at the cathode. It is desirable that the experimental cells used in polarographic studies should be conducted in a nonoxygen medium because the oxygen in the air can be reduced polarographically, resulting in erroneous test results (Brett, 1993; Brown et al., 1964; Gosser, 1994; Greef et al., 1990; Hart, 1990; Ozkan, 2012; Ozkan et al., 2015; Palacek et al., 2005; Scholz et al., 2010; Vire & Kauffmann, 1994; Wang, 2006; Zhang et al., 2008).

In CV, the electrode potential is varied linearly between the initial electrode potential E i and the final electrode potential E f for examining the reactivity of the electrochemical system thoroughly over a large range of potentials with a single sweep. CV applications are widespread and make possible the analysis of a number of drugs (Brett, 1993; Brown et al., 1964; Gosser, 1994; Greef et al., 1990; Hart, 1990; Ozkan, 2012; Ozkan et al., 2015; Palacek et al., 2005; Scholz et al., 2010; Vire & Kauffmann, 1994; Wang, 2006; Zhang et al., 2008).

In linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), the current is measured as a function of the potential, which is swept linearly in time. Like CV, LSV is a commonly used technique for drug analysis. Staircase voltammetry and LSV or CV are very similar in many ways. In staircase voltammetry, reverse potential scans can be applied, similar to CV; thus, a cyclic staircase voltammogram is formed. This voltammogram has the same peaked-shape as a linear scan voltammogram. However, differences exist between these techniques. For example, a staircase voltammetry voltammogram has several current—potential points, while LSV or CV creates a continuous current versus potential curve. Another technique is stripping voltammetry, based on measuring the amount of the substance deposited on the electrode at a preconcentration step, then stripping it with any voltammetric method, that is, returning it back to the solution. In anodic stripping voltammetry, trace element analysis at the ppt level can be performed. In addition, 4–6 elemental analyses can be made at the same time. In this method, the analyte in the solution is deposited on the electrode by applying a suitable negative potential within a given time period. The resulting layer is then stripped from the electrode surface by applying a positive potential. The peak current obtained is proportional to the concentration of the drug in solution (Brett, 1993; Brown et al., 1964; Gosser, 1994; Greef et al., 1990; Hart, 1990; Ozkan, 2012; Ozkan et al., 2015; Palacek et al., 2005; Scholz et al., 2010; Vire & Kauffmann, 1994; Wang, 2006; Zhang et al., 2008). Cathodic stripping voltammetry can be used for the identification of substances that form water-insoluble compounds together with drugs. By applying a positive potential to the mercury electrode, an insoluble layer is formed at the electrode surface. Then, a negative potential is applied to dissolve the accumulated substances and to dissolve them (Brett, 1993; Brown et al., 1964; Gosser, 1994; Greef et al., 1990; Hart, 1990; Ozkan, 2012; Ozkan et al., 2015; Palacek et al., 2005; Scholz et al., 2010; Vire & Kauffmann, 1994; Wang, 2006; Zhang et al., 2008).

In adsorptive stripping voltammetry, adsorption on the surface of the analyte or electrode during the deposition process or chemical attachment to the modified electrode by a chemical reaction occurs. The analyte that is deposited on the surface is then determined by scanning with a technique, such as DPV or SWV, either in the positive or negative potential direction. The height of the obtained peaks is proportional to the concentration. It is frequently used in the field of pharmacy, because this method can be administered in very small quantities (Brett, 1993; Brown et al., 1964; Gosser, 1994; Greef et al., 1990; Hart, 1990; Ozkan, 2012; Ozkan et al., 2015; Palacek et al., 2005; Scholz et al., 2010; Vire & Kauffmann, 1994; Wang, 2006; Zhang et al., 2008).

In electrochemical nanosensors, the key point is the working electrode that is used. Mercury electrodes are widely used in voltammetric studies in electrochemical methods. They have several advantages over other microelectrodes. The first is the high excess of hydrogen formation from hydrogen ions and voltage; this is not the case, without any inhibition from the acidic solutions it can be reduced to a lot of materials. The second advantage is that a new metal, the creation of the surface, the aging of the electrode, or past experiments do not affect its reproducible current-voltage curves, regardless of how the electrode was previously used. The third advantage of the drop electrode is that any application of potential gives repeatable average currents. It can be used over a wide range of cathodic potential ranges until −2.0V. Two negative features of the mercury drop electrode have also been investigated. One of these properties is the easy oxidation of the sludge, which is much higher than +0.4 V (vs. SCE) oxidation (halogens and some ions). Moreover, the potential of the electrode should be more negative than 0.0V; if stronger, it is not possible to reduce the oxidizing substances because such substances oxidize the mercury. The second negative characteristic of the drop electrode is its sensitivity, which is more than 10−5 M, which does not allow analysis of dilute solutions. At lower concentrations, the diffusion current is equal to the residual flux or less. Built-in basic flow corrections lead to major errors and reduce sensitivity significantly. Most of the residual currents are not faradaic and more than a few microamperes cannot be reduced to a minimum level. The main sources are the load current and noise currents and the device is derived from cell cycles and other electrical sources. The effect of its resources increases with the growth of the cell's resistance.

There are two types of study.
•
In the first, the electrode is fixed and vibration and convection is avoided.
•
In the second operation, the electrode is rotated or vibrated at a constant speed; or the electrode is kept constant and the solution is mixed regularly under optimum conditions.

In both cases, the electrode and solution are moving against each other. Although mercury electrodes were commonly used in electroanalytical methods before, for nanosensors solid electrodes are mostly used nowadays. There exist other different kinds of electrodes, such as carbon-based electrodes, glassy carbon electrodes, diamond electrodes, carbon paste electrodes, screen-printed electrodes, and paper-based electrodes.

Carbon-based electrodes are commonly used in electroanalytical chemistry because of their excellent properties, such as chemical inertness, low background current, low cost, broad potential window, rich surface chemistry, and properties for many difficult applications. Electroanalytical methods can detect new drug compounds with carbon-based electrodes (Süzen, Ateş-Alagöz, Demircigil, & Özkan, 2001).

Glassy carbon is a class of nongraphitizing carbon that has circular cross section and a copper cladded part. It is also known as vitreous carbon. The use of solid glassy carbon electrodes in electrochemistry and surface modification has become increasingly important over the years. Due to oxidation and contamination occurring at the surface of the GC electrode, the electrode reaction mechanism can be different, so that the solid electrode surface can be modified to prevent this contamination. The electrode surface is prepared by subjecting the organic precursors to a series of heat treatments at temperatures up to 3000°C (Brett et al., n.d.; Wang, Cepría, & Chen, 1996). Glassy carbon electrodes are the most common transducers in electrochemical studies. They are preferred in many applications in many different fields.

Diamond is a polymorph of the element carbon. It has good mechanical, thermal, and chemical properties that find applications in the environmental area (Pleskov, 2002). At the beginning, pristine diamond electrodes were used with their excellent crystal structures. Boron was added to diamond (boron-doped diamond, BDD) in recent years due to its extraordinary chemical resistances. These electrodes are more highly resistant against anodic stresses and strong acidic media than classical carbon and other superior-to-metal electrodes (Briones et al., 2015; Brycht, Kaczmarska, Uslu, Ozkan, & Skrzypek, 2016; Kurbanoglu & Ozkan, 2018; Lee, Lee, Phillip, Lee, & Lee, 2010; Pleskov, 2002; Wang et al., 1996; Zhan et al., 2016). The carbon paste electrode can very easily be modified. Carbon paste electrodes were first proposed by Adams in 1958. Modified carbon paste electrodes are prepared via various organic binders that do not mix with water and graphite powder. Hence, easily renewable surfaces can be obtained (Adams, 1958; Ibrahim, Temerk, & Ibrahim, 2017; Kalcher, 1990; Lima, Calaça, Viana, & Pessôa, 2018; Manjunatha, 2017).

While the modifying agent is generally composed of a single material, the paste may contain two or more components. The modifier is simply a mixture of graphite and paste. The carbon paste of the modifier composition in this case usually ranges from 10% to 30%. This ratio depends on the modifier capacity of the active part of the material on the paste surface (Kalcher, 1990). Carbon paste represents a suitable matrix for the incorporation of appropriate modifying moieties.

Thick film technology using a screen printing procedure is a simple and fast method for mass production of disposable electrochemical sensors (Hart & Wring, 1994). Screen-printed electrodes, in particular, are easy to use and portable, with simple and inexpensive fabrication techniques. Materials, such as polyester, Teflon, and polycarbonate, are used in such electrodes. The working electrode can be prepared by dispersion of the graphite powder into a suitable polymer and the solvent is printed on the materials by the screen printing technique. Single-use screen printed electrodes have advantages, such as small sizes, and can be prepared with a large number of materials and fewer materials (Adkins & Henry, 2015; Andreescu, Barthelmebs, & Marty, 2002; Hart & Wring, 1994; Kröger, Turner, Mosbach, & Haupt, 1999; Materon, Wong, Klein, Liu, & Sotomayor, 2015; O’Halloran, Pravda, & Guilbault, 2001; Renedo, Alonso-Lomillo, & Martínez, 2007).

Miniaturized electrochemical devices that have mobile properties have attracted increasing interest because of their sensitivity, small sample volume (several μL) requirement, insensitivity to light levels, independence of human interpretation of color, and the fact that they are easily interfaced with the web. In recent years, paper-based electrodes have been printed due to their flexibility, availability, reduced thickness (230μm), and easy maneuverability. Paper has also been used as an integral component in electrochemical cells. An integrated paper-based device that includes an accurate reference electrode would enable the full range of electrochemical measurements in a low-cost and disposable format (Adkins & Henry, 2015; Eaton & Smith, 1997; Nie et al., 2010; Sun et al., 2004; Tortorich, Shamkhalichenar, & Choi, 2018; Wu et al., 2016; Yao et al., 2017).

Nanomaterials can be described as materials that are between 1 and 100nm in size. The use of nanomaterials in designing chemical nanosensors is very common due to their physical, chemical, thermal, optical, magnetic, plasmonic, and catalytic characteristics. Moreover, their ability to speed up the signal transduction and improve the sensitivity of the chemical nanosensor is the reason of choice. Depending on the type of material, nanomaterials can be classified as carbon-based and noncarbon-based nanomaterials. Carbon-based nanomaterials include graphene, fullerene, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers, and carbon nanohorns. Noncarbon-based nanomaterials that are generally derivative of metallic nanomaterials include metal oxide nanoparticles, nanocomposites, nanowires, nanoneedles, and nanorods (Kurbanoglu & Ozkan, 2018; Lao et al., 2007; Rao, Chintamani, Müller, & Cheetham, 2004; Vo-Dinh, Cullum, & Stokes, 2001; Xu et al., 2012; Zhao et al., 2004).

Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene, diamond, nanofibers, and nanowires, have attracted great attention due to their unique properties, which include high surface-to-volume ratio, low density, low weight, high electrical conductivity, robust mechanical strength, and biocompatibility (Chen & Chatterjee, 2013). They can be used in many application fields, especially in electrochemical sensors for biological compounds, drugs, and various materials (Chatterjee & Chen, 2012; Chen & Chatterjee, 2013).

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs), derived from bottom-up chemical synthesis, have been regarded as an important nanostructured material since their discovery in 1991 by Iijima (1991). CNTs are the most important current mediators in sensor and fuel cell studies due to their mechanical and electrical features. In the transmission made by CNTs, 10–100 times more current than the transmission of polyphenylene-based chains occurs. The thermal conductivities of CNTs are two times that of diamond and 1000 times that of copper, which can carry more electricity. CNTs can increase the electron transfer reaction. Hence, these materials are preferred in many sensor developments. CNTs are usually classified according to the number of graphene walls that make them up. Carbon nanotubes may be formed by a single graphitic layer, or multiple coaxial layers. A single graphitic layer formation is called a single-walled carbon nanotube (SWCNT) and, when multiple coaxial layer formation occurs, it is called a multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWCNT). SWCNTs are wrapped into cylindrical tubes with diameters in the range between 0.4 and 2.5nm. They have excellent properties in terms of both chemical and physical features, which are used to the advantage of many applications (Hong, Diao, Antaris, & Dai, 2015). In sensor studies, SWCNTs can decrease the overpotential, which promotes sensitive detection of materials or molecules (Chatterjee, Wen, & Chen, 2013). The outer diameter of a MWCNT varies between 2 to 100 nm and the inner diameter can be 1–3nm with 0.2μm length (Bhagath Singh et al., 2012). In nanomaterials, quantum effects become more dominant as the size decreases. Due to the unique structures of CNTs, they are used in many applications (Allen, Kichambare, & Star, 2007; Karadas & Ozkan, 2014; Kurbanoglu, Dogan-Topal, Uslu, Can, & Ozkan, 2013; Kurbanoglu & Ozkan, 2018; Zhao, Gan, & Zhuang, 2002).

For example, in their study, Reddy et al. developed a nanosensor for the determination of epinephrine in pharmaceutical tablets and urine using a carbon nanotube hybrid nanocomposite ensemble. They used carbon nanotube-chitosan nanocomposite, which was characterized by using Raman and scanning electron microscopy. Within the 0.05 and 10μM linear range of epinephrine, the limit of detection was calculated to be 30nM (Fig. 1 ). (Koteshwara Reddy et al., 2017)

Graphene is more preferred for novel nanosensor development with high detection limits due to its high active surface area, exceptionally low noise, and electrical properties (Choi, Lahiri, Seelaboyina, & Kang, 2010; Hu et al., 2010; Novoselov et al., 2004).

Fullerenes occupy one of the first places in carbon-based materials. They are composed of carbon, in the form of a hollow sphere, ellipsoid, or tube. Fullerene structures are also known as the great carbon cages. The most widely known are the C60, C70, and C84 forms. These materials have an antioxidant property. Thus, there are many applications in the medical industry. They are used in solar cells and fuel cells because of the hydrogen storage feature, and they are also used together with tungsten disulfide in bulletproof vests. The fullerene C60 molecule is the most stable structure, with a highly symmetrical structure (Taylor, Hare, Abdul-Sada, & Kroto, 1974). The unique physical and chemical properties of fullerenes has led many scientists to predict several technological applications. C60 fullerenes are insoluble in aqueous media and aggregate very easily (Prato, Farmaceutiche, & Europa, 1997). Other studies related to carbon-based nanomaterials are summarized in Table 1 .

Metallic nanoparticles can be synthesized electrochemically in six steps. In the first step, oxidative dissolution of the sacrificial M bulk anode occurs. In the next step, M
n + ions migrate to the cathode part followed by metal atoms formed at the cathode by the reduction process. In the third step, metal particles increase in size by nucleation and growth mechanisms. In the next step, colloidal protective agents are used to arrest and stabilize the growth or nucleation process. In the last step, nanosized metal colloids are precipitated (Bönnemann & Richards, 2001).

In their study, Jamali et al. suggested a novel nanosensor based on a Pt:Co nanoalloy ionic liquid carbon paste electrode for the determination of vitamin B9. The square wave voltammetric method was used for the determination of submicromolar amounts of vitamin B9. Within the linear range of 1.0×10−7 to 5.0×10−4 M, vitamin B9 was detected with a limit of detection value of 4.0×10−8 M (Fig. 2 ).

Quantum dots (QDs) are nanocrystals with 1–10nm size that are formed by cationic groups II–VI mixed with anionic groups II–V (Frigerio et al., 2012; Yin et al., 2010). The increasing number of recently reported electrochemical sensors in which fabricated QDs are employed is mainly due to the inherent possibility of using them as electrochemical signal tracers to exploit the oxidation potentials of the metal ions forming them. This can be attributed to some of the characteristics of QDs, such as a large surface-to-volume ratio, good interfacial properties with high surface reaction activity, high electron-transfer efficiency, excellent biocompatibility, and feasibility for surface modification (Chen et al., 2016; Khene & Nyokong, 2011; Li, Li, Yang, Qu, & de Harrington, 2013). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged recently as a new class of QDs. The massless relative nature of graphene energy distribution results in the unique properties of quantum dots. They are zero-dimensional, closely packed, honeycombed graphene nanosheets that are less than 100nm in size (Li et al., 2013). GQDs are formed by magnetic potential. Additionally, GQDs are endowed with low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high water solubility, and excellent electrical conductivity (Zhou, Qu, Zeng, Zhou, & Shi, 2014).

In a recent study, Shie et al. developed a dual-mode nanosensor based on carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles for the determination of glutathione (GSH) in human plasma. With the designed nanosensors, the authors proposed that the detection of glutathione was as low as 50nM (Fig. 3 ).

Nanowires can be conductive or semiconductive materials with nanometer dimensions (1–100nm). Quantum mechanical properties are important at nanosize scales, so that nanowires are called quantum wires. They are accepted as one-dimensional particles because the length-to-width ratio is 1000 or more. Nanowires offer unique opportunities and an interdisciplinary area of research in science and technology (Morales & Lieber, 1998). Inorganic nanowires and nanocrystals exhibit unique electrical properties that can be exploited for sensing (Hu, Odom, & Lieber, 1999; Huang & Choi, 2007; Morales & Lieber, 1998; Patolsky & Lieber, 2005; Patolsky, Zheng, & Lieber, 2006; Wanekaya, Chen, Myung, & Mulchandani, 2006).

In one study, the authors suggested an aptamer/AuNP-based sensor in conjunction with a fluorescing single-stranded DNA aptamer for the sensitive detection of bisphenol A. The authors suggested a fluorescent and colorimetric chemosensor for BPA. The developed method yielded a detection sensitivity of 9pgmL−1. The suggested study allowed the detection of bisphenol A in a small fraction extracted from thermal paper with a high specificity (Lee et al., 2018) (Fig. 4 ).

Polymers have a wide range of application areas (sophisticated electronic measuring devices, such as sensors) because of their chemical and physical properties. Increasing interest in the replacement of receptors with semisynthetic ligands, such as various proteins and aptamers, and wholly synthetic analogues, such as molecularly imprinted polymers, has made a great contribution to the sensors. Molecular imprinting offers a route to the creation of specific and selective cavities in a 3D-polymeric network, which are complementary not only to the size and to the shape of a target species, but also provide interaction points and a coordination sphere around the template molecule. Polymer-based receptors attract attention due to possibilities for mass production, simplicity, improved shelf life and cost, and because they potentially minimize batch-to-batch variations in performance. Also, automated production with predetermined features for both single- and multianalytical detection is another attractive opportunity created by polymer-based biosensors, with considerable potential for improved personalized healthcare (Tortorich et al., 2018). Polymer-modified electrodes are more preferred than metal electrodes, because they have a lower flux at a wider potential range and because the uncertainty in response to the electrode current is reduced to a minimum.

The use of nanomaterials for the development of chemical nanosensors has attracted growing interest in recent years. New types of nanomaterials are widely used in chemical nanosensing for their unique mechanical, thermal, electrical, and physical properties. Nanomaterial-modified electrodes coupled with different techniques are commonly used. Chemical nanosensors can be designed using different kinds of nanomaterials, such as carbon-based (fullerene carbon nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamond, etc.), metal-based (gold, silver, platinum nanoparticles, etc.) and/or polymer-based. In this chapter, chemical nanosensors classified as carbon-based nanomaterials in chemical nanosensors, metal-based nanomaterials in chemical nanosensors, and polymer-based chemical nanosensors were discussed along with their applications in pharmaceutical analysis with different types of samples.
